1: A fifth speech opens with the appointment of the tabernacle's artisans. The transition is significant: all previous instructions have specified what to make; now God identifies who will make it, and grounds their ability not in professional training alone but in divine equipping. The craftsmanship of the tabernacle is as much a theological act as its design.
2: Betzalel ben Uri ben Chur (בְּצַלְאֵל בֶּן אוּרִי בֶן חוּר) — the name Betzalel means 'in the shadow of God' or 'in God's protection' (be-tzel El, H1212). Uri means 'my flame/light'; Chur possibly 'white/noble'. The genealogy traces to Judah (1 Chronicles 2:19-20), the royal tribe — the artisan of the sanctuary belongs to the tribe from which the king will come. Qara'ti beshem (I have called by name) — divine personal appointment, the same formula used of the prophets (Isaiah 45:3-4 for Cyrus; cf. John 10:3 for the shepherd who calls his sheep by name).
3: vamalei oto ruach Elohim bechochmah uvivunah uveda'at (I have filled him with the Spirit of God, in wisdom and understanding and knowledge) — the first explicit artistic Spirit-filling in Scripture. Ruach Elohim (H7307+H430, Spirit of God) appears at creation hovering over the waters (Genesis 1:2) and in prophetic contexts; here it descends upon a craftsman. The three cognitive gifts: chokmah (H2451, wisdom — skill-knowledge, experiential mastery), tevunah (H8394, understanding — analytical discernment), da'at (H1847, knowledge — intimate, relational knowing). This triad appears again in Proverbs 3:19-20 as God's own faculties in creation; Bezalel's endowment mirrors the Creator's cognitive equipment, making his artisanship an act of co-creation.
4: lachshov machashavot (to devise/design devised plans) — machashevet (H4284, a thought/plan/design) is the intellectual dimension of craft: conceiving the form before execution. Charosh zahav uvekheseph uvenchoshet (skilled work in gold, silver, and bronze) — charosh (H2796) is the craftsman who incises, engraves, or cuts. The three metals correspond to the tabernacle's three zones: gold (inner sanctuary), silver (outer sanctuary fittings), bronze (court).
5: uvacharshat even lemallo (and in cutting stone for setting) — gem-cutting for the high priest's garments (Exod 28). uvacharshat ets la'asot bechol melacha (and in carving wood, to work in every craft) — Bezalel's giftedness is not specialized to one material; the Spirit of God equips him comprehensively. Melacha (H4399, work/craft) is the same word used for God's creative work in Genesis 2:2-3 and for the prohibited Sabbath labor — the artisan's melacha for the tabernacle is sacred work at the intersection of human skill and divine design.
6: Oholiav ben Achisamach lematteh Dan — the second artisan, from Dan (the northern tribe; Bezalel from Judah, the southern). The pairing spans the breadth of Israel. Oholiav means 'my father is my tent' (ohel + av) — the tent/tabernacle is embedded in his name. uvechol-lev chakham (everyone who is wise-hearted) — chacham-lev (H2450+H3820, wise-hearted) is the standard phrase for skilled artisans throughout the tabernacle sections (Exodus 28:3; Exodus 35:10; Exodus 36:1,2,4). Lev (heart) in Hebrew thought is the seat of will and intelligence; the wise-hearted artisan integrates technical skill with oriented purpose.
7: The list of objects to be made — ohel moed ve'et ha'aron la'edut (the tent of meeting and the ark of testimony) — restates the scope of the tabernacle commission in summary. The word kaporet (mercy seat/cover) appears alongside the ark; the artisan mandate encompasses the most holy object. The same catalog runs through Exod 35-39 when the actual execution is narrated, confirming that these appointments in Exodus 31 are the authorization for what will be accomplished there.
8: The table and its utensils, the pure (gold) lampstand and all its utensils, the incense altar — the divine instruction cycles back through the furniture catalog in reverse order, as though the artisan is being handed a full set of blueprints by reviewing the contents of the tent systematically. Menorat hatahorah (the pure lampstand) — tahor (pure) modifies the lampstand consistently; it belongs to the category of pure gold objects in the inner sanctuary.
9: The altar of burnt offering and all its utensils, the laver and its stand — completing the outer court furnishings. The scope is total: everything from the most holy inner ark to the outermost court bronze belongs to the Spirit-filled artisans' mandate. The tabernacle is not partially designed by God and partially improvised; it is comprehensively commissioned.
10: bigdei hasrad (the vestments/service garments) — serad (H8278) appears only in the tabernacle sections and means the plaited or woven garments used for ministry service. The priestly garments are included in the artisan commission: making the garments is making the priesthood visible. Bigdei haqodesh leAharon hakohen uvigdei banav lekhaheno (the holy garments for Aaron the priest and the garments of his sons to serve as priests) — garment and office are inseparable.
11: shemen hamishchah ve'et qetoret hasamim (the anointing oil and the fragrant incense) — the two prescriptions just given in Exodus 30 are now included in the artisan mandate. Bezalel's team does not only build furniture; they compound the sacral substances. Kekol asher tsivitika ya'asu (according to all that I have commanded you, they shall do) — the artisan's freedom is bounded by divine specification; creativity serves faithfulness, not self-expression.
12: The Sabbath legislation is inserted here, between the artisan commission and the giving of the stone tablets. Its placement is architectural: the construction of the tabernacle is the dominant human project, and the Sabbath is given as a limit that even tabernacle-building cannot override. Rabbinic tradition derives the 39 categories of prohibited Sabbath labor from the categories of work used to build the tabernacle (m. Shabbat Exodus 7:2).
13: ach et Shabbatotay tishmeru (nevertheless/but you shall keep my Sabbaths) — the adversative ach signals a qualification upon all that precedes: however urgent and holy the tabernacle construction, Sabbath observance is non-negotiable. Ki ot hi beini uveinekhem (for it is a sign between me and you) — ot (H226, sign/token) marks the Sabbath as a covenant marker analogous to circumcision (Genesis 17:11) and the rainbow (Genesis 9:13). The sign-function of the Sabbath makes Israel's week itself testify to God's creative authority and covenant relationship.
14: ushemertem et hashabbat ki qodesh hi lachem (you shall keep the Sabbath, for it is holy to you) — the Sabbath's holiness is explicitly for Israel's benefit: lachem (to you/for you). The death penalty for Sabbath violation — mot yumat (he shall surely be put to death) — classifies Sabbath-breaking with the gravest covenant violations. Mechallalah (whoever profanes it) — chalal (H2490) means to pierce, to defile, to make common what is sacred; the vocabulary of defilement, usually reserved for the sanctuary, applies here to time.
15: Shabbat Shabbaton (שַׁבַּת שַׁבָּתוֹן, a Sabbath of solemn rest/complete Sabbath) — the doubled form intensifies: not just a Sabbath but a complete, total, absolute rest. The same construction appears for the Day of Atonement (Leviticus 16:31) and the Jubilee year (Leviticus 25:4). Qodesh laYHWH (holy to the LORD) — the day belongs to God in the same categorical sense that the anointing oil and incense belong to God: possessed by, not merely dedicated to, the divine.
16: veshameru venei Yisrael et hashabbat (the sons of Israel shall keep the Sabbath) — corporate, generational obligation. La'asot et hashabbat ledoroteikhem berit olam (to observe the Sabbath throughout their generations as an everlasting covenant) — berit olam (H1285+H5769, everlasting covenant) is the formula for unconditional, permanent covenantal commitments: the covenant with Noah (Genesis 9:16), with Abraham (Genesis 17:7,13,19), and now the Sabbath. The Sabbath is not a cultural practice but a covenant identity marker with permanent status.
17: ki sheshet yamim asah YHWH et hashamayim ve'et ha'aretz (for in six days the LORD made the heavens and the earth) — the Sabbath law is grounded in creation theology, as in the Decalogue version (Exodus 20:11). Uvayom hashevi'i shavat vayinnafash (and on the seventh day he rested and was refreshed) — shavat (H7673, to cease, to rest) is the root of Shabbat. Nefash (H5314, was refreshed/took breath) — the nifal of nefesh (soul/breath); the idiom is 'he caught his breath,' an anthropomorphic expression for God's Sabbath rest that grounds Israel's rest in divine pattern. This is the only place God is described as vayinnafash — refreshed — on the Sabbath.
18: luchot ha'even (לֻחֹת הָאָבֶן, tablets of stone) — two tablets; their material signals permanence and legal authority. ketuvim be'etsba Elohim (written with the finger of God) — etsba Elohim (H676+H430, finger of God) is the exact phrase the Egyptian magicians used to describe the gnats plague (Exodus 8:19). There the phrase expressed awe at an act of power; here it describes the writing medium of the covenant itself. The tablets written by God's own finger are the documentary evidence of the Sinai covenant, and their shattering in Exodus 32 is the severest covenantal crisis in Israel's history to this point.
